q: Coordination in animals is the process involved in the detection of stimuli by receptors and subsequent actions of the effectors.
The endocrine system is involved in the transmission of nerve impulses.
The main purpose for living organisms respond to certain stimuli is to ensure the continuity of life or survival of the organisms.
Homeostasis is the regulation of the internal environment of living organisms.
The central nervous system is composed of the brain and the spinal cord which are located at the central axis of the body.
The forebrain comprises the cerebrum, thalamus and hypothalamus.
The hindbrain consists of the cerebellum, pons and medulla oblongata.
The brain is fragile and has a gelatin-like consistency, protected by the skull and meninges.
Cerebrum accounts for more than four fifths of the total weight of the brain and acts as the centres for intellect, learning and memory.
Thalamus acts as "the great relay station" of the brain and provides connections between various parts of the brain.
Hypothalamus contains neurones that control blood pressure, heart rate, body temperature, and basic drives such as thirst and hunger.
Midbrain is involved in processing information from sensory neurones in the eyes, ears, and nose.
Cerebellum receives information from specialized sensors called proprioceptors located within skeletal muscles and joints.
Medulla oblongata coordinates many reflexes and automatic bodily functions that maintain homeostasis.
A cross section of the spinal cord reveals both white matter and grey matter, where the butterfly-shaped core is made up of grey matter.
The somatic nervous system is under voluntary control and services the head, trunk, and limbs.
The vagus nerve is a cranial nerve that has branches to the throat and larynx, but also connects to many internal organs.
The autonomic nervous system is under automatic, or involuntary control, and maintains homeostasis by adjusting to variations in environment.
Reflex arcs are simple connections of only three neurones to transmit messages.
The sympathetic neurones release a neurotransmitter called norepinephrine, which has an excitatory effect on its target muscles.
The parasympathetic nervous system uses the neurotransmitter acetylcholine to control organ responses.
Glial cells nourish the neurones, remove their wastes, and defend against infection.
Dendrites are short, branching terminals of dendron that receive nerve impulses from other neurones or sensory receptors.
The axon conducts impulses away from the cell body and can range in length from $1 mm$ to $1 m$.
Functions of the myelin sheath include protecting the nerve fibre, supplying nutrients to axon, and acting as an insulator to the nerve impulses.
Multipolar neurones have several dendrites and a single axon and are found in the brain and spinal cord.
Bipolar neurones have a single main dendrite and a single axon and are found in the inner ear, retina, and olfactory area.
A neuromuscular junction is a synapse between a motor neurone and a muscle cell.
Synaptic vesicles release their contents into the synaptic cleft, which is only about $0.02 \mu$ wide, by exocytosis.
Upon reaching the postsynaptic membrane, neurotransmitters bind to specific receptor proteins to depolarize the membrane.
If the neurotransmitter is inhibitory, the receptor will trigger potassium channels to open, allowing potassium ions to flow out.
Acetylcholine excites the muscle cell membrane, causing depolarization and contraction of the muscle fibre.
Inadequate production of dopamine is linked to Alzheimer's disease.
Serotonin regulates temperature and sensory perception and is involved in mood control.
Endorphins act as natural painkillers in the brain and affect emotional areas of the brain.
